quiz_questions.question_text: Which compound is ionic?

options.text: CO2 (carbon dioxide molecule)
H2O (water / dihydrogen monoxide)
NaCl (sodium chloride / table salt)
CH4 (methane / natural gas compound)

quiz_questions.explanation: NaCl is ionic because it is formed between a metal (sodium) and a nonmetal (chlorine) through electron transfer. The other compounds are covalent, formed between nonmetals.

quiz_questions.question_text: How can you tell if a compound is ionic or covalent from its formula?

options.text: Ionic compounds always contain oxygen in their formula
Ionic compounds contain a metal and nonmetal; covalent contain only nonmetals
Covalent compounds always start with hydrogen in their formula
There is no way to determine bond type from the formula alone

quiz_questions.explanation: Generally, compounds with a metal and nonmetal are ionic, while compounds with two nonmetals are covalent. This is the quickest way to determine bond type from a formula.

quiz_questions.question_text: What naming system uses Greek prefixes like di-, tri-, and tetra-?

options.text: Ionic compound naming with metal cations
Covalent compound naming with nonmetals
Acid naming with hydrogen compounds
Organic compound naming with carbon chains

quiz_questions.explanation: Greek numerical prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-, tetra-, etc.) are used in covalent compound naming to specify the number of atoms of each element.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the name of CaCl2?

options.text: Calcium dichloride (using covalent naming rules)
Calcium chloride (using ionic naming rules)
Dicalcium chloride (using mixed naming rules)
Calcium(II) chloride (using transition metal rules)

quiz_questions.explanation: CaCl2 is calcium chloride. Since calcium is a metal and chlorine is a nonmetal, this is ionic. Ionic names don't use Greek prefixes, and calcium always has a +2 charge so no Roman numeral is needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the name of N2O4?

options.text: Nitrogen oxide compound
Nitrogen(IV) oxide compound
Dinitrogen tetroxide compound
Tetranitrogen dioxide compound

quiz_questions.explanation: N2O4 is dinitrogen tetroxide. Both elements are nonmetals, so this is covalent and uses Greek prefixes: di- for two nitrogen and tetra- for four oxygen.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which compound would use Roman numerals in its name?

options.text: NaCl because sodium is a metal element
FeCl3 because iron has multiple charges
CO2 because carbon is the first element
H2O because hydrogen is a nonmetal element

quiz_questions.explanation: Roman numerals are used for ionic compounds with transition metals that can have multiple charges. Iron can be Fe2+ or Fe3+, so FeCl3 is named iron(III) chloride.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the correct name for MgO?

options.text: Magnesium monoxide (covalent naming)
Magnesium oxide (ionic naming rules)
Monomagnesium oxide (mixed naming)
Magnesium(II) oxide (Roman numeral needed)

quiz_questions.explanation: MgO is magnesium oxide. Magnesium is a metal and oxygen is a nonmetal, making this ionic. No prefixes are needed, and magnesium always has a +2 charge so no Roman numeral is needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: The compound P4O10 would be named using which system?

options.text: Ionic naming because it contains oxygen
Acid naming because it starts with P
Covalent naming with Greek prefixes because both are nonmetals
Organic naming because it has many atoms in it

quiz_questions.explanation: P4O10 contains phosphorus and oxygen, both nonmetals, so it is a covalent compound named with Greek prefixes: tetraphosphorus decoxide.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for iron(III) oxide?

options.text: FeO (iron with +2 charge)
Fe2O3 (iron with +3 charge)
Fe3O2 (three iron, two oxygen)
FeO3 (one iron, three oxygen)

quiz_questions.explanation: Iron(III) means Fe3+ and oxide is O2-. To balance charges: 2(+3) + 3(-2) = 0, giving the formula Fe2O3, which is rust.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a covalent compound?

options.text: KBr (potassium bromide compound)
CaF2 (calcium fluoride compound)
LiCl (lithium chloride compound)
SO3 (sulfur trioxide compound)

quiz_questions.explanation: SO3 is covalent because both sulfur and oxygen are nonmetals that share electrons. The other compounds contain metals and nonmetals, making them ionic.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the key difference between ionic and covalent nomenclature?

options.text: Ionic uses Greek prefixes while covalent uses Roman numerals for naming
Covalent uses Greek prefixes while ionic uses metal name plus nonmetal with -ide
There is no difference since both types use exactly the same naming rules
Ionic compounds always end in -ate while covalent compounds end in -ide

quiz_questions.explanation: Covalent naming uses Greek prefixes (di-, tri-, tetra-) to indicate atom numbers. Ionic naming uses the metal name followed by the nonmetal with -ide, sometimes with Roman numerals.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a Roman numeral indicate in an ionic compound name?

options.text: The number of atoms of the metal present in the formula
The charge (oxidation state) of the metal cation in the compound
The number of bonds formed between the two elements present
The total number of electrons the metal has in all shells

quiz_questions.explanation: Roman numerals in ionic compound names indicate the charge of the metal cation. For example, iron(III) means Fe3+ and copper(II) means Cu2+.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a polyatomic ion?

options.text: A single atom that has gained or lost one or more electrons
A group of covalently bonded atoms that together carry a charge
A neutral molecule that does not carry any electrical charge
An atom that has more neutrons than protons in its nucleus

quiz_questions.explanation: A polyatomic ion is a group of two or more atoms that are covalently bonded together and carry an overall electrical charge, such as sulfate (SO4 2-) or nitrate (NO3-).

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of compound is Na2SO4?

options.text: A covalent compound with Greek prefix naming rules
An ionic compound containing a polyatomic ion (sulfate)
An organic compound containing carbon-based molecules
A metallic compound with delocalized electrons present

quiz_questions.explanation: Na2SO4 (sodium sulfate) is an ionic compound. Sodium (Na+) is the cation, and sulfate (SO4 2-) is a polyatomic anion. It is named using ionic naming rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define the term 'binary compound' in chemistry nomenclature.

options.text: A compound that contains exactly two total atoms in its formula
A compound that is made up of exactly two different elements
A compound that can exist in two different states of matter
A compound that has exactly two chemical bonds in its structure

quiz_questions.explanation: A binary compound is composed of exactly two different elements, whether ionic (NaCl) or covalent (CO2). The term binary refers to two elements, not two atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'nomenclature' mean in chemistry?

options.text: The process of balancing chemical equations in reactions
The system of rules for naming chemical compounds and formulas
The method used to measure the mass of chemical elements
The arrangement of elements on the periodic table of elements

quiz_questions.explanation: Nomenclature is the systematic method of naming chemical compounds according to a set of rules. Different types of compounds (ionic, covalent, acids) each have their own naming conventions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a cation?

options.text: A negatively charged ion formed when an atom gains electrons
A positively charged ion formed when an atom loses electrons
A neutral atom that has not gained or lost any electrons
A molecule that is shared between two bonded atoms equally

quiz_questions.explanation: A cation is a positively charged ion formed when an atom (usually a metal) loses one or more electrons. In ionic naming, the cation is always named first.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an anion?

options.text: A positively charged ion formed by losing electrons
A negatively charged ion formed by gaining electrons
An atom that has no charge and is electrically neutral
A pair of electrons shared between two bonded atoms

quiz_questions.explanation: An anion is a negatively charged ion formed when an atom (usually a nonmetal) gains one or more electrons. In ionic naming, the anion name ends in -ide.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the Stock system refer to in chemical naming?

options.text: Using Greek prefixes to show atom counts in covalent compounds
Using Roman numerals to show the charge of transition metal cations
Using common names for well-known chemical compounds like water
Using prefixes like hydro- and suffixes like -ic for naming acids

quiz_questions.explanation: The Stock system uses Roman numerals in parentheses after the metal name to indicate its charge, such as iron(III) chloride for FeCl3 or copper(II) sulfate for CuSO4.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the term for a transition metal that can have more than one charge?

options.text: A monovalent metal with only one possible charge state
A multivalent metal with more than one possible charge state
A nonmetal element that forms only covalent bonds always
A noble gas element that does not form any bonds at all

quiz_questions.explanation: A multivalent metal can form ions with different charges. For example, iron can be Fe2+ or Fe3+, and copper can be Cu+ or Cu2+. Roman numerals distinguish them.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student sees the formula KBr and is asked to name it. The student writes 'potassium monobromide.' What correction should be made?

options.text: The name should be potassium(I) bromide with a Roman numeral
The name should be potassium bromide without any prefix since it is ionic
The name should be monopotassium bromide with prefix on both elements
The name is correct as written because all compounds need prefixes

quiz_questions.explanation: KBr is an ionic compound (metal + nonmetal), so Greek prefixes are not used. The correct name is simply potassium bromide. Prefixes are only for covalent compounds.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has two bottles: one labeled CuCl and the other CuCl2. How should each be named to tell them apart?

options.text: Copper chloride and copper dichloride using covalent prefixes
Copper(I) chloride and copper(II) chloride using Roman numerals
Copper monochloride and copper bichloride using older naming rules
Both are just called copper chloride since they have the same elements

quiz_questions.explanation: Since copper is a transition metal with multiple possible charges, Roman numerals are used: CuCl is copper(I) chloride (Cu+) and CuCl2 is copper(II) chloride (Cu2+).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is given the name 'sulfur hexafluoride' and asked to identify the bond type. What should the student conclude?

options.text: It is ionic because the name contains the element sulfur
It is covalent because Greek prefixes indicate two nonmetals bonded together
It is metallic because fluorine is found in metallic compounds
It could be either type since the name alone does not indicate bonding

quiz_questions.explanation: The use of Greek prefixes (hexa-) is a clear indicator of covalent naming. Both sulfur and fluorine are nonmetals, confirming the covalent bond type.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student needs to write the formula for aluminum oxide. She knows aluminum is Al3+ and oxide is O2-. What formula should she write?

options.text: AlO (one aluminum, one oxygen atom)
Al2O3 (two aluminum, three oxygen atoms)
Al3O2 (three aluminum, two oxygen atoms)
AlO2 (one aluminum, two oxygen atoms)

quiz_questions.explanation: To balance Al3+ and O2-, you need 2 Al3+ (total +6) and 3 O2- (total -6) to reach a neutral compound: Al2O3. This is the criss-cross method.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student encounters the compound PbO2 and needs to name it. Lead (Pb) is a transition metal. What is the correct name?

options.text: Lead oxide (without any Roman numeral designation)
Lead dioxide (using covalent Greek prefix naming)
Lead(IV) oxide (using Roman numeral for the charge)
Lead(II) oxide (using the wrong Roman numeral)

quiz_questions.explanation: PbO2 is ionic (metal + nonmetal). Each O is 2-, so two O = 4-. Lead must be 4+ to balance. The name is lead(IV) oxide. Greek prefixes are not used for ionic compounds.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student looks at the compounds NaBr and PCl3. She needs to name both correctly. Which names are correct?

options.text: Sodium monobromide and phosphorus trichloride
Sodium bromide and phosphorus trichloride respectively
Sodium(I) bromide and phosphorus(III) chloride respectively
Monosodium bromide and monophosphorus trichloride

quiz_questions.explanation: NaBr is ionic (metal + nonmetal) so it is sodium bromide with no prefixes. PCl3 is covalent (two nonmetals) so it uses prefixes: phosphorus trichloride.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is given four compounds: NaCl, CO2, Fe2O3, and N2O5. How many are ionic and how many are covalent?

options.text: All four are ionic compounds because they contain nonmetals
Two are ionic (NaCl, Fe2O3) and two are covalent (CO2, N2O5)
All four are covalent because they all contain oxygen atoms
Three are ionic and only one is covalent in this group

quiz_questions.explanation: NaCl (Na metal + Cl nonmetal) and Fe2O3 (Fe metal + O nonmetal) are ionic. CO2 (C + O nonmetals) and N2O5 (N + O nonmetals) are covalent.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is asked to name the compound Cr2O3. Chromium is a transition metal with multiple possible charges. What is the correct name?

options.text: Dichromium trioxide (using covalent Greek prefix rules)
Chromium(III) oxide (using ionic naming with Roman numerals)
Chromium oxide (without specifying the charge of chromium)
Chromium(II) oxide (using the incorrect Roman numeral value)

quiz_questions.explanation: Cr2O3 is ionic. Three O2- ions total -6, so two Cr ions must total +6, meaning each Cr is 3+. The name is chromium(III) oxide using the Stock system.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is given an unknown compound that has a high melting point, conducts electricity when dissolved in water, and forms crystals. What type of compound is it most likely?

options.text: A covalent compound with weak intermolecular forces between molecules
An ionic compound with strong electrostatic attractions between ions
An organic compound with carbon chains that are very long
A noble gas compound that does not form any chemical bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: High melting point, electrical conductivity in solution, and crystal formation are characteristic properties of ionic compounds, which have strong electrostatic attractions between ions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student must decide how to name SnO2. Tin (Sn) is a metal with multiple charges. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Name it tin dioxide using covalent Greek prefix naming rules
Name it tin(IV) oxide using ionic naming with a Roman numeral for Sn's charge
Name it tin oxide with no Roman numeral since tin has only one charge
Name it tin(II) oxide since tin always has a +2 charge in compounds

quiz_questions.explanation: SnO2 is ionic (metal + nonmetal). Two O2- = -4 total, so Sn must be +4. Since tin has multiple possible charges, a Roman numeral is needed: tin(IV) oxide.
